title: EDSI 2025/11/06
displayTitle: 2025-11-06 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - EDSI 2025/11/06
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee

chunks.text: Thank you so much. Thank you for the wait. No problem. Yeah. Um, thank you. So, um, it's a joint hearing with, uh, DIA and Public Service Commission. So if you would like to start with some opening remarks, you want to do it, um, in your individual organization capacity or together, that's totally fine.

Thank you. And then we'll go onto questions. Thank you. Uh, thank you, um, Madam Chair and committee, and, uh, I'll be brief. My name's Paul James. I'm the Government Chief Digital Officer and the Chief Executive of Department of Internal Affairs. Um, and it's a pleasure to be here. I'm joined by Murray Davy, um, who is the Chief Digital Officer for Department of Internal Affairs, and my colleague from the Public Services Commission.

But you introduce yourself, Hugo. Um, thank you, Madam Chair. My name's Hugo Batal. I'm the Deputy Chief Executive Strategy, policy and Integrity at the commission. Thank you. And so in terms of just some brief introductory remarks, um, so as Government Chief Digital Officer are mandated by the Public Services Commission under Public Services Legislation, uh, as a system lead, and that means that I have responsibility to work across the public service, not just down inside my own agency, um, across digital.

So practically what that looks like is we have a focus on helping ensure there's a consistent vision and direction for digital across the public sector. Uh, we work around standards, policies and guidance to help consistency and coherence across the public sector. And we also put in place tools and support services to support the public sector.

We don't take away from individual agency or see accountability, uh, to deliver the services and take responsibility for their own digital, uh, service and systems and applications. But we look to provide value in a collective. And so, uh, for example, uh, we have a, uh, collection of services, things like, uh, a cloud framework agreement with a major company.

So public service agencies come in, uh, through our commercial constructs to access those services. It means that we scale up the purchasing power of the crown through those contracts and get, um, benefits around pricing, but we can also put in, um, standards around security and other things that are important like that. So that's a system lead role. And in terms of ai, I've also been asked to lead, um, uh, the adoption of AI for the public service.

And so take a similar approach to what we do around digital, but with ai, which there's clearly close connections. And so, um, my focus is on the adoption of AI by the public service. So, uh, the customer base for us is the public service. So that's who we focus on as agencies. Of course, we think deeply about New Zealanders because that's the public services customers.

Um, but our starting point is the agencies that we work with, and it focuses around the adoption of ai. I didn't wanna say a lot more than that. I thought that, uh, it would be best to be guided by the committee in terms of the questions that you might have. Great. Thank you. Anything that you would like to add? Um, hug, Uh, no, uh, the only two other elements other than, uh, the designation of, um, the government Chief Digital Officer under the Public Service Act, uh, the commission, uh, is also, uh, obviously the, um, employer on behalf of the Crown

of Chief Executives also has a responsibility for developing capability of its, of its leaders. And we do that through the Leadership development Center, which is club funded by, uh, all agencies. Uh, and then, um, you know, we, uh, also have responsibilities around performance and accountability and, um, and for, uh, integrity and ethics.

So promoting, uh, account, uh, promoting integrity, uh, transparency and accountability, that's, that's our main areas for, that are relevant for the committee today. That's great. Thank you, Dr. Hamish. Yeah. Excellent. Um, thank you. I appreciate talking about the adoption of AI and public service. How much of it is actually being driven down, uh, from, from top?

And how much is actually people actually developing these tools and using the tools, whether it be, um, bundled with other, other software? That's a great question. I think it's really important that we have both. Uh, why it's really important we have both is that, uh, like any tool, um, AI needs to be applied to what's the objective here?

What's the business purpose, what's the benefit? What are we trying to achieve? And that will, uh, most often come from up, if you like, from down from people who are working, have accountabilities, responsibilities. Uh, but what we also know when there's new technology coming in and the public service needs to respond and adapt, rather than everyone learning a hundred percent by themselves, totally a new and a fresh, how can we actually accelerate or support by bringing in consistent standards, templates, policies, guidance, uh, to create both a platform

and a head start for people. Um, over time, I'd expect us to mature a little bit too. And by that I mean we'll start putting in place common tools, some common technology solutions for people to use, but at the moment, we're not at that point. Mm-hmm. So, yeah. Do, do you have any other technologies which this approach has been used to, um, for its adoption and the Public service?

Yeah, I mean, this is how we work generally across, across digital. So we try and we put in place frameworks, expectations, common approaches, but agencies know their responsibilities better than anyone else. They've got their own accountabilities. Um, you know, I'm not gonna be responsible for social services. That's gonna be the responsibility of the Ministry of Social Development. Um, and technology needs to be deployed in context and around a particular, uh, need and purpose and to improve a New Zealanders experience or outcomes.

And so it's getting the balance between how do you have something that is coherent across the public service, but is actually responsive to the immediate needs and responsibilities of that agency and the new Zealanders that they support Who've been doing. Thank you. Um, I'm curious to know whether you've got a sense of which agencies of organizations are the highest users and which are much lower users.

We have a sense, um, and we have a sense because, uh, I hope the committee will be familiar with an annual survey that we've started around AI adoption across the, uh, broadly the 46 core agencies that are our mandate. Um, and so we get some, uh, data from that. We also have a community of practice operating. Uh, we've just got several hundred people, uh, come online around that once a month or so.

And so in that conversation, we get a sense as well. So it is, as we see across other technologies mixed in terms of where agencies are up to. Um, A, CC and IR are quite, um, um, proactive and they're quite engaged in ai, uh, themselves. And there are other organizations. It tends to reflect their digital maturity as well.

Either their data isn't structural set up or they're not cloud hosting, and it's harder to start to deploy the technologies. So they are earlier in terms of what they might be doing. So we see variety at the moment, but I'd identify a, C, C and IR as two organizations that are more advanced in their, um, exploration of the value or utility of Ai. Yeah, sure. And at the opposite end of the spectrum, the lower uptake or the, the least use, Um, always a bit wary about pointing out agencies that might not be, uh, there.

But, um, for some organizations, um, perhaps, uh, technology is a bigger part of their, uh, business. Maybe some of the population agencies, agencies that might represent, um, views into the public sector. They might not be, uh, technology to deliver services isn't as much of what they do. So they're not necessarily, uh, into AI as much.

But having said that, they are talking to us often about the policy or standards or design. So we've had quite an engagement with, for example, around ai. Uh, 'cause they see both concerns of bias, um, but more they see opportunities for actually improve their outcomes. If this technology is adopted in effective ways that make it easier for the communities that they represent, Is there any resistance?

So using, using, using resistance in any areas. Um, what we see is, um, a broad kind of, um, if you like coder around that is we see, uh, sufficiency. So people are, no, we are, okay. We, we don't think we need ai. We're all right. They might not say that, but we see that in, in the patterns.

Uh, we see, uh, concern, we're not sure about this, we, but worried about this. We see confusion, which, which tool we use, where, where do we go? So I think those are all natural responses when you have a new technology, which is still moving quite fast and probably a bit immature. Um, and so our real focus is, um, a big part of our focus is actually this foundational, um, policies, guidance, and education for leaders so that they can start to build their own knowledge and comfort so they can see.

So I think we've got a duty to the public to make sure that we are aware of what this technology is and thinking about how it could improve delivery of services. So we are really encouraging people. I, I've stood in front of CES and Deputy Cs and said, in five years time, it's hard to imagine you wouldn't look back and see that you've used AI more now than you did five years ago.

Mm-hmm. So if that's the case, what do you need to start doing today? Prepare for that. And often it's just about your own education and your own ability to, um, operate and then start to see what might be possible for the services that you're accountable for. Mm-hmm. Thank you. Right. Um, I'll ask a quick question. We were discussing about hallucinations, um, in AI and in your role of building capability in our public system.

So this morning, MBI told us that, see there needs to be a human in the loop. So just making sure that the information is correct. How is that being put in place, that kind of, uh, system kind of control, um, in, in public services? Uh, just curious to know that. Yes, absolutely.

Look, I think, um, what we see is that, that that's definitely the case that you can see bias and hallucinations in these systems. And we will all read a media of examples and instances of that, the actual deployment of AI into the public sector. The one is very limited. So, um, it, it's into administrative tasks around summarizing information or it's, uh, some, um, administrative efficiencies.

And so, uh, we don't see, um, the modern AI tools being deployed into core delivery operations or responsibilities where those hallucinations or biases would present real risks and challenges. So we don't see that yet. However, um, the framework that we've, um, adopted and have put out across the public service around adoption is based on six OECD principles.

And it's all very much aimed at trying to make sure that, uh, there's careful, responsible, ethical and assured adoption of ai. So I think a lot of the risks can be seen if, um, you go through very deliberate processes around it and are very, uh, prudent about how you do it.

So, um, Murray, here's objective also for DIA, he'll be horrified if we just deployed AI straight into our system without thinking about it. So it is creating the right technical environment, so sandboxes, places that are safe to start to experiment and see what happens, uh, and then making sure we've got all the right governance and controls across it.

So I, I think we should, uh, be mindful of the risks, but at the moment, we are very early in the deployment, and so we're not seeing those emerge. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. I might add, uh, there's also controls that you can apply. So, uh, approaches like retrieval, augmenta, augmented generation, uh, approaches such as retrieval, augmented generation, or rag as it's called that enable you to point a, a particular AI at a particular knowledge base and say you can only provide answers based on that knowledge base.

So that helps you to make sure that you're using factual information and you can be more confident about the output that you're gonna get from that. So, um, so That's More like a customized kind Of Yes, absolutely. And so, you know, if we are using an AI internally within the organization, uh, then we'll do that. We'll, if we're using it for HR processes, for example, uh, we'll point it at a set of HR policies and processes, say that's the only knowledge base from which you can provide the answers.

And that way we can be confident that the answers that it's providing are accurate, um, and similar approaches can be used for in other use cases. That's good. Uh, any question from Dr. David Wilson? Yeah, thank you. Um, it's really in instructive for us this morning. We, we can, well, at least I came to the conclusion that perhaps this was disaggregated and sporadic across the whole public service.

And, um, so now you've told me that it's a system wide approach. I'm very pleased to hear that. Um, it's very good. You said that you were looking at adoption capability, performance, accountability, and ethics. Also very pleased to hear you, um, done that. 'cause when we've bashed into this area, that's what keeps coming up.

Um, and so for us as policy makers, we'll be looking at areas that will bump into ethics all the time. And so my question really is how can we learn from what you are learning is, I know it's constrained by what you can do within the public service, but, you know, how can we pick up on what's happening here?

I think, um, what we recognize is everyone's learning in this area at the moment. Um, and it's not just public sector, it's private sector too. Mm-hmm. So, oh, yes. Uh, I think it, it's really encouraging Very fast and pushing against boundaries way faster than we Areye. Yes, they are. And um, so I was in, uh, Singapore in September this year.

I go there every year for a digital government exchange with about 12 countries and talking with the countries there about ai, but also had a round table with some large Singapore and businesses around what they're doing around ai. Uh, and they were clear that, um, they're not getting a return on investment at the moment with ai that most of their, uh, proof of concept aren't working. Um, but they're still highly committed to ai. Yeah.

So they, they see the promise, but what that told me is that the private sector is not cracked at yet either. That they're still on, on that journey. I think in terms of your point about how we share learnings, so that's why we are really focused on, uh, executive education, but also I mentioned that community of practice. So bringing people together who are doing this to swap notes, to share insights, uh, I think that's the power of the public services actually, to be able to bring, uh, people from quite different organizations together.

Uh, and to learn from each other. Um, we have to learn from outside the public service too. Yeah. So, um, we've got a, um, an advisory body that we've set up just to, for us to able to talk through, um, uh, which is New Zealand people, but from, um, private sector and some public sector expertise. So we need to find ways that we can bring those different views and perspectives in, and that will pick up ethics, uh, um, as well.

So as, um, policy policymakers, legislation, can we come to a place and ask what are the ethical dimensions around this policy, this evangelous new idea? Well, um, we are always happy in, uh, in the right channels to be able to provide our views. And I think in, in digital government, it's a, it's an area of responsibility where there is a high level of openness and bipartisanship is my experience.

And so, um, minister Collins, who we worked to is always very supportive of us engaging with people. So, do you think, um, so any, any closeups, any members? So just, um, uh, thinking about IP with the use of ai, the issues of plagiarism and all, so we like kind of covering ourselves for, for these kind of issues.

It's a really good question. And again, I, um, think it's an area where the answer isn't clear yet. And so that most often comes through, um, LMS and the training data sets that they're being built on. And so, uh, how are they getting access to that data? And you can see, again, stories of people feeling like the content they've produced has been consumed by these, uh, tools and that they're training and learning off them.

We, we are not building our own LLM, uh, from New Zealand Public Service. We don't have the capital or the expertise to do that. I think as we see things develop over the next couples of the technology, we will start to see potential for us to either tailor, um, something or potentially, uh, even look at how we might invest in a small language model.

There'll be opportunities, I think, for us to do something that has got the New Zealand context and, um, that will help with those points. But for that wider question around plagiarism, we see the risks, uh, and the training data sets that things are built on at the moment, we are really subject to that market. Um, so it's not something we're looking closely at.

If there's anything to that, Um, I asked, oh, you go first. Yes. Thank you. Um, just looking specifically at the strategy, and I know there's been a lot of, um, commentary post the release of the strategy. There's an acknowledgement up top that it was, um, that AI was used to help generate it.

And there are a couple of, um, uh, team observations made, one around, um, potential hallucinations within it, um, and another around, um, basic spelling error with it. Did, uh, any, any comment on that? Um, that's a, a strategy that we were involved in creating.

So we worked alongside mb it's obviously oriented at the whole of country whole of economy. Um, we haven't really put a strategy in place for the public sector. We, we made the decision that actually what's really needed is guidance is, um, principles, um, for the public sector because it's moving so quickly.

So, um, so it's hard for me to comment on the, um, uh, questions around hallucinations or, or spelling mistakes and something that while we were involved in it ultimately was an MB lead across that. So my apologies. But You, do you think there's scope or a need or a similar strategy for you to have a similar strategy but without hallucinations or scope?

Um, we, we have talked about that. So we have a strategy for digital public service, uh, which is our anchor strategy. It's been in place since 2019. It's largely vision and principles based. So we still think it works. We had a lock at and went, it's still not, not too bad. But what we really heard from the public sector was crying out for guidance. Um, so that people wanted guidance about how to think about ai, how to approach it, what are the principles or parameters.

And potentially we could wrap all this up in a strategy at some point in the future. But at the moment, that's really what, um, we're being asked for by the public service. Interesting. Talking with other jurisdictions and, and, uh, countries about their strategies and in some cases their legislation. The feedback I've been getting, uh, from other countries is they're actually moving away a little bit from strategies or legislation.

'cause the technology's moving so fast, it's very hard to keep up with. And they're thinking that actually a principal's based approach that can then respond to whatever changes in the future is potentially where they need to head from now. So, um, as I said, really guidance frameworks policy is where our focus is for the next 12 to 18 months.

Mm-hmm. So I'll ask this next question. So, because AI can, uh, I mean access any, uh, any websites, so with our information going on websites, uh, how are we looking at ensuring that it's not accessible by any of the AI tools that we don't want it to be?

So is it like, work on some kind of firewall? So because it could be information that is just relevant to New Zealand and maybe there is some information that might be sensitive. Obviously sensitive information won't go our website, but just, you know, if it is stored in a way where AI can access, so how, I mean the it, there, there is this assurance that it'll not go out through any AI tool.

That's a great question. So, um, in our starting point guidance for people is if you publish something on a website, you need to expect that it is public and anyone will be able to see it and access it. And that includes AI tools. So, but there is a risk, those tools might take that material and might either hallucinate it or introduce other bias or connect it up with other information so we can see that risk.

We think in an AI future, it's gonna be really important for you to be trusted, known, and authoritative sources of information. And so one of our, um, current, uh, areas of focus is a chat bot for the.gov got NZ web domain. So that would run across all websites that we're dot gov nz.

So, uh, we've completed proof of concept of that, uh, went pretty well. So we've just gone out to market. We've had three vendors come in and we're just at the final stages of making a choice. So we intend to put in place a chat bot that runs across the.gov nz domain, and then if we can say that's a New Zealand government product and, um, avoid hallucinations and some of the other challenges we can see, uh, we'll respond quickly if we have any issues, but give people a place they can go that's branded, certified, and trusted as opposed to, I mean,

Google was a real key entry point into New Zealand services digitally for, for a long time and still is. So people jump into their search engine and Google search engine into how do I do this with the New Zealand government? And that takes to a particular place on the digital footprint of government. So you can see that AI tools will start to do the same thing. I I don't think we can avoid that, but it's how do we create visibility for the public of the trusted channels into this?

Yeah, yeah. Which is government owned and constructed. So it can be reproduced, but it's just that besides the source, yes, obviously website anybody can access, but I'm just thinking about when it is reproduced And, and I think this is where we see the risks around, uh, DeepFakes and what AI technology offers and that how do you, uh, water market or give people confidence about the providence and the source of that information.

And so trust, which is central to digital and government, how do we, uh, ensure that's maintained with ai? And there, there must be something about the brand and so people knowing where it comes from that it is, uh, authoritatively from the New Zealand government and that they can have trust for these reasons, because it's been built, uh, with respect to the framework we've got, which has got ethics and responsibility and transparency of its heart, et cetera.

Mm-hmm. And do we use a lot of intranet, intranet? Um, have you had an answer to that? Um, uh, at the moment, those are some of the areas we're starting to experiment with internally is, is looking at, uh, with agencies though most agencies will have their own intranet internally with their own internal information.

So how do you make it easier for your own staff to get that information and access that information? So a couple of areas that we are looking at, um, we're looking at can we, uh, use AI to surface information very quickly out of a knowledge base for our staff to help to respond to customer queries, uh, and also for our own staff. So that HR example that I used before, rather than having to go to one of our HR people and ask them a question, can you actually ask a a chat bot, which can lock up our processes and guide our staff, which saves everybody internally time.

So those are some of the areas we're starting to pilot now That I'm asking because that, uh, in my view will help, uh, confine the information to the organization And absolutely in, in both those cases, we're confining information to known and good sources, um, so that we know that, you know, the answers our staff will be getting back are, are solid.

Yeah. New question Lu Davidson. It's really a SAP off some of the answers to your, your questions Yeah. Around the trust issue. Yes. And you've talked about some of the potential steps that could be taken. Why do you think we rank so low in trust currently? Like New Zealand's near bottom and Global trust and ai?

Um, look, I'll make an observation about, uh, digital services, but Hugo, you might like to contribute on that too. So, um, Kiwi's count the survey that Public Service Commission has, which runs I think quarterly, um, across a range of services really we, we've got some questions in the last 18 months around digital, uh, the latest survey that came out, 86% of people who access services digitally of the New Zealand public Service have trust in them that's higher than accessing services in physical channels or omnichannels.

So people who access services digitally have greater trust in them than people who access services in other ways. Now, that's potentially because they've made a choice if they wanna access it digitally. So I understand that, but I do, that gives us confidence that when they access the digital services have got a high level of trust in that digital service, which I think is of real value. I know that there's been, um, static or declining levels of trust in, in government and public services across time.

I think we actually held pretty well through COVID. Uh, yes. So, um, actually trust in the public service, uh, through COVID actually increased to the highest level ever. So it's 69%, it's come back a little bit. It's at, uh, I think around 60 61%, which is higher than when the series started.

And about, uh, five to 10 percentage points higher than the private sector. I think your question is about sort of concerns about ai. Um, prior to answering that though, um, the New Zealand public service actually consistently ranks, uh, in the top five, uh, in the world, uh, on, on most of the main measures for integrity and lack of corruption.

And that's a position we want to maintain. But I think your, what you were, um, talking about the surveys showing, uh, concerns amongst the New Zealand public and even within the public service about the use of AI and the sort of unknown and the ethics of that. And I think there's a genuine as issue, uh, as the public service adopts ai.

Um, and that's why it's being done in a very careful, responsible way, starting with adopting the best practice frameworks, uh, that have been issued, uh, and why we're taking quite a sort of balanced approach towards seeing the opportunities as well as the risks. One area also internationally to your earlier question, uh, what we're seeing internationally is an investment in leadership and capability and training.

And that's been a consistent message. And we've been working with the GCDO, uh, the LDC, the Leadership Development Center has, uh, developed two courses. So there's a masterclass for chief executives and there's also a foundational learning program, uh, which is about four to five modules on AI that goes through ethics.

Actually it's available, uh, if you Google, uh, Google, sorry, Uh, LDC foundational courses. Um, I'm doing that course myself. Um, it's really helpful. It's really easy to understand. It goes through sort of ethics, the productivity, sort of the possibilities, the risks.

Uh, it also talks about things that are quite specific to our context. So treaty wa, all those things are kind of covered and, um, that's been tested with a, a first cohort of senior leaders and we're working with GCDO to think about the forward program for that. But we think investment and leadership is going to be really important to make sure we sort of, we are, uh, dig, uh, savvy in this area, so we understand it.

I probably, um, didn't do your question justice either then. So, um, one of the conundrums that exist in digital government across Westminster style democracy, so we work pretty readily with other jurisdictions, is, um, around privacy and information. For example, people very nervous about government holding all the information about them or sharing information about them.

Very frustrated that we keep asking them for the same information all the time. And, uh, both are true and it's a potentially a little bit like that with AI is that there's real concerns in trust about government and AI generally, but then real frustrations about the services and adopting or adapting to AI to provide a better quality of service. And what we have learned is if we do a really good job with the service, we build trust from that.

And so again, that just emphasizes Hu's point about the choice we've made, about starting with, um, adopting. We have to move, we have to adopt it, but we want to be careful and deliberate and do it in ways that, um, works for people. Then we think that we'll build trust. Yeah. Great. So some subs off. Okay, there's a number there now, um, on the trust piece, and you talked about 86% trust in the digital interactions or transactions.

I'm curious to know whether, as you also pointed out that that's from people carrying those out. Have you seen a shift with the uptake of AI across the public service? Have, have you seen a shift in people wanting to move away from transacting digitally as a result? No. No. So we, these questions only went in last year.

Um, so we, I, we wouldn't have a long series of data, but there's continuing increase in digital uptake of services. But I would also make the point, we haven't really got AI as the service entry point yet for services. So people aren't, um, using an assistant, an AI assistant to help them access or navigate services. I think we're probably sort of few years away from that, um, being widespread.

So, so we haven't seen that yet, but there's probably not really an opportunity for people either. Yeah. Right. Okay. And to come to the masterclass and the, and the training side of things, um, are you able to give us an idea of the numbers of people who've played that to date, but also what that, what that looks like?

Um, they, what you'd expect to have through that in the next six months, 12 months? Um, it was the initial pilot I believe. Um, I'll, I'll have to, um, confirm the numbers. I think, uh, there were, um, I think 14 chief executives in the first masterclass. So it was just a testing, uh, testing the concept and they were around, uh, sorry, uh, I will make sure I get you correct numbers.

I think it was around 45 senior leaders who attended the, uh, first pilot sort of, uh, uh, who've been going through the first sort of foundational learning. Um, and, uh, we're still working on sort of what, you know, the, the forward work program to 2027. So we started with CEEs, not bad, uh, and we went to deputy chief executives ran two master classes.

Deputy chief executives got about 30 in each of those, sorry, we had 50 people went through the, uh, the um, LDC program that was really to test and develop. And now we've put it online so that you don't have to go in as part of a cohort. It's just there for people to use now that we've proved it. So, but we, we also held a, an event in parliament, which was really by the public sector for the public sector.

We had about 300 leaders from across the public service there for a day, uh, talking about ai. But we are looking to make sure everybody engages in some shape or form with this training over time. I dunno. Thank you. Um, this has been really helpful and interesting. Um, I'm interested in the impact, um, in the public service on, uh, like for service users and people who work within the public service floor, particularly for young people.

Um, it's something that's not in the GCDs, um, briefs, cabinet papers. It's not, um, something that this part of the, uh, MDs document that NDDR strategy, but presumably we are working on this through a point at which there are thousands of parts jobs gone. Um, the job marketers, the young people, 15,000 with the election out worked, um, and then moving into a job market, which is very, very changed that they, you know, you don't have, um, the lower level analytical jobs, the running jobs.

Um, what, who is the system steward to ensure that young New Zealanders have access to the skills that they'll need to drive the AI developments? Um, that is an OECD principle around data use that, um, people come along for the journey and that, um, we are not robots, we're decoded of the robots who is in charge to make sure that, um, young New Zealanders have access to what they need to do to get the skills they need to, to be able to use these tools in a professional setting.

Yeah, that's, um, again, a very good question and it's a conversation topic with other jurisdictions when we talk with them too. So at the moment we are not seeing any employment displacement from, from ai. Um, it's, uh, at the moment an assist tool. Um, it might be taking steps out for people, meaning that they can do things in a shorter amount of time.

Um, but it's been, I guess, well traversed in, um, worldwide really about what the impacts of this might be. And there's just lots of questions about that. Um, we don't have, uh, really good sites at the moment about what that might mean or be.
